title: Higher-order Symmetric Quantum Mpemba Effect in Fragmented Systems
authors: Sreemayee Aditya, Sara Murciano, Xhek Turkeshi
abstract: A quantum system can restore a broken symmetry faster the more strongly it initially breaks it, an anomaly known as the quantum Mpemba effect. Whether this effect survives once conservation laws fragment the Hilbert space into exponentially many disconnected Krylov sectors has remained open. We address this question for circuits and Hamiltonians with simultaneous charge and dipole conservation, the paradigmatic setting for strong Hilbert-space fragmentation. Combining a replica tensor-network formulation for charge and dipole-conserving gates, which reaches the annealed Rényi-2 entanglement asymmetry up to $L=128$, with Hamiltonian simulations and an exactly solvable dissipative model, we uncover a higher-order symmetric quantum Mpemba effect: the charge and dipole asymmetries each display Mpemba-like crossings on parametrically distinct timescales. Resolving the state into frozen and active Krylov sectors reveals the mechanism: frozen fragments retain a finite asymmetry that obstructs full restoration, while active fragments host the relaxation responsible for the crossings. Fragmentation thus does not preclude the quantum Mpemba effect but reshapes it into frozen memory and active-fragment relaxation, providing a framework for the Mpemba phenomenology of higher-moment symmetries.
summary: This work explores the survival of the quantum Mpemba effect in highly fragmented quantum systems governed by multiple conserved quantities. By analyzing random circuits, deterministic Hamiltonians, and toy models, the authors demonstrate that fragmentation does not eliminate the effect but rather reinterprets it as a combination of memory retention (frozen sector) and active relaxation dynamics.
main_problem: The study investigates whether the quantum Mpemba effect, related to symmetry restoration, survives when a system's Hilbert space is fragmented by multiple conservation laws.
main_result: A higher-order symmetric quantum Mpemba effect (SQME) is found, which reshapes the standard QME into contributions from 'frozen memory' and 'active-fragment relaxation.'
method: The research employs replica tensor-network formulations for random circuits, Chebyshev polynomial expansions for Hamiltonians, and analytical solutions for dissipative toy models.
model_or_system: The analysis covers three settings: a charge/dipole conserving random circuit; a pair-hopping Hamiltonian; and an analytically tractable dissipative pair-flip model. These systems are characterized by strong Hilbert-space fragmentation due to conserved charges (U(1) charge and dipole moment).
key_observables: Entanglement asymmetry ($\Delta S^{(n)}_O$), which serves as the diagnostic for the Mpemba phenomenon.
important_parameters: System size ($L$, up to 128 in RTN), conservation laws (charge $Q$ and dipole moment $P$), and dissipation rate ($\gamma$).
main_assumptions: The random circuit analysis relies on replica tensor networks for averaging; the dissipative model uses a Markovian approximation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of results across three settings (RTN, Hamiltonian simulation, Lindblad dynamics), showing QME crossings and the separation into frozen/active dynamic sectors.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the QME in increasingly complex models: first random circuits using RTNs, then deterministic Hamiltonians via Chebyshev methods, and finally a simple dissipative toy model to isolate the mechanism.
why_interesting: It provides a general theoretical framework showing how fundamental non-equilibrium phenomena like the Mpemba effect persist even when quantum dynamics are severely constrained by multiple conservation laws and fragmentation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic and summary of our study.

Figure 2. Von Neumann and R´enyi-2 asymmetries associated with charge and dipole for an initial pure spin-1/2 tilted ferromagnetic state, computed for a boundary subsystem of size LA = 3 as a function of the tilting angle. In all cases, the analytical results are in excellent agreement with the numerical data.

Figure 3. Replica tensor network for the charge- and dipole- conserving random circuit. The contraction gives the R´enyi-2 purity-like quantities shown for L = 12 and LA = 3. Each in- teger time step applies one staggered layer of Haar-averaged four-site transfer operators, shifted by one site relative to the previous step under OBCs. The lower panel shows how Haar averaging of (U ∗⊗U)⊗2 gives the four-site transfer tensor Ti,...,i+3, subject to the appropriate charge and dipole con- straints. Here O is the untwisted swap for purity, while O = Q and P are the charge- and dipole-twisted swap contractions for charge and dipole-resolved purity, respectively.

Figure 4. Charge and dipole asymmetry dynamics in a charge- and dipole-conserving Haar-random circuit with L = 128. (a-b) Charge and dipole second-R´enyi asymmetries for tilted ferromagnetic initial states with θ = 1.25 and θ = 1.45, computed for a boundary subsystem of size LA = 6 using a replica tensor-network method with bond dimension χ = 512. The curves exhibit a quantum-Mpemba-effect-like crossing, followed by slow late-time relaxation to a nonzero saturation value, as shown in the log-log insets. (c-d) Crossing times tQ M and tP M vs LA. For small LA, the data showcases tQ M ∝L2.09 A and tP M ∝L1.49 A ; for larger LA, the crossings disappear within the accessible time window.

Figure 5. Frozen and active contributions to the R´enyi-2 entanglement asymmetries for a boundary subsystem in the charge- and dipole-conserving random circuit. The data are obtained for system size L = 16 and subsystem size LA = 3, starting from tilted ferromagnetic initial states with θ = 1.25 and θ = 1.45. Panels (a,b) show the frozen-sector charge and dipole asymmetries, ∆S(2) Q,fr and ∆S(2) P,fr, while panels (c,d) show the corresponding active-sector asymmetries, ∆S(2) Q,act and ∆S(2) P,act.
